text: The surgeon is performing a dissection at the hiatus during a parasophageal hiatal hernia repair, focusing on separating tissue layers around the esophageal opening.
The surgeon dissects anteriorly on the right crus of the diaphragm, carefully separating the esophagus from surrounding tissue.
The surgeon's view is partially obstructed by a large vessel crossing through the pars flaccida, affecting the visualization of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses indocyanine green fluorescence angiography to assess a vessel that may be a replaced left hepatic artery arising from the left gastric artery.
The surgeon clamps a vessel and injects three milligrams of indocyanine green (ICG) dye, preparing for fluorescence angiography to assess the vessel's characteristics.
The ICG dye visibly fills the lungs, followed by its distribution into the peripheral tissues.
The surgical field shows well-vascularized tissues, but the left liver remains unilluminated despite the use of endocyanin green fluorescence angiography.
The surgeon observes that the CAUT-VIII and right liver appear bright green under fluorescence, while the left side of the liver shows no apparent perfusion.
The surgeon unclamps the vessel previously clamped for ICG injection, resuming surgical work on the liver.
The surgeon attempts to free tissue in the pars flaccida to gain length on the vessel, carefully manipulating the surrounding structures.
The left lobe of the liver lights up green, indicating perfusion, after the vessel has been released.
